title: Premature greying of hair
excerpt: Why hair greys early, the pitta-heat link Ayurveda describes, and the calm, cooling habits the old texts suggest, with honest words on what oiling and diet can and cannot do.
body_html:
the grey that comes too soon

You spot the first silver strand somewhere near the temple, then a few more, and you are nowhere near the age you thought this happened. Maybe a parent went grey early too, and a quiet worry sets in. This is a common, everyday concern, and Ayurveda has thought about it for a very long time, calmly and without alarm.

The tradition has a name for it: palitya, the greying or whitening of hair. The texts treat it as a natural change with age, but they also notice when it arrives early, and they have a clear idea about why.

cooling habits may slow new greying and support the hair you have, but no oil or diet turns silver hair back to black

the pitta-heat link

Ayurveda connects early greying mainly to pitta, the fiery principle of heat and transformation in the body. The Sushruta Samhita, in its section on the structure of the body (Sharirasthana), describes how excess heat reaching the scalp can scorch the hair and turn it grey, much as heat changes the colour of anything it touches over time. The Ashtanga Hridaya echoes this, placing palitya among the disorders rooted in aggravated pitta (Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana, on diseases of the head and hair).

What raises that heat? The texts point to familiar things. Anger, grief, and constant worry are described as kindling inner heat. So are very spicy, sour, salty, and fried foods eaten often, along with too much sun and too little rest. Modern life delivers all of these generously, which is why the tradition reads early greying less as a scalp problem and more as a signal from inside, that the body's heat has run a little high for a while.

There is also the matter of inheritance, and Ayurveda does not pretend otherwise. When greying runs strongly in a family and starts young, the texts would see a natural tendency at work. The honest expectation here is gentle: cooling habits may slow new greying and support the hair you have, but no oil or diet turns silver hair back to black. Anyone promising that is selling hope, not Ayurveda.

cooling habits the texts suggest

The everyday approach is to cool the system and care for the scalp, none of it dramatic. First, oiling the head. The Ashtanga Hridaya recommends abhyanga, regular oil massage, as part of a healthy daily routine (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 2), and oiling the scalp with a cooling oil is a soothing, time-honoured place to begin. Coconut-based oils, often infused with amla or bhringaraj in the classical hair-oil tradition, are the customary choice.

Second, lean on amla. The Bhavaprakasha Nighantu describes amalaki (amla) as a foremost rejuvenating (rasayana) fruit, gentle on all three doshas and especially calming to pitta. This is a traditional attribution about overall vitality and cooling, not a tested cure for greying, but eating amla in season, fresh or as a simple home preparation, is an easy and well-loved way to bring cooling in.

Third, cool the plate and the mind. The tradition would favour eating less of the very spicy, sour, and fried, choosing cooling foods, drinking enough water, and not skipping rest. Because palitya is tied to heat born of anger and worry, a few quiet minutes of slow breathing or meditation each day sits naturally alongside the oil and the food. It costs nothing and supports the steady routine (dinacharya) Ayurveda values.

when to see a doctor

Early greying is usually just a natural tendency shaped by heat, stress, and inheritance, but it can occasionally point to something that needs attention, such as a thyroid issue, vitamin B12 deficiency, or other nutritional gaps. If your hair greys very suddenly or in patches, if it comes with tiredness, weight changes, or other symptoms, or if it simply worries you, please see a doctor or a qualified vaidya for proper checking. This article is general information drawn from classical texts, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care.

Try This Today

  • a cooling scalp oiling: Once or twice a week, warm a little coconut-based oil, ideally one infused with amla or bhringaraj, and massage it gently into the scalp. Leave it on for a while, then wash with lukewarm, not hot, water. This follows the old abhyanga custom of Ashtanga Hridaya.
  • bring amla into your week: Eat fresh amla in season, or add it to your food in a simple home form. The Bhavaprakasha Nighantu prizes amla as a cooling rejuvenator that calms pitta, the dosha linked to early greying.
  • cool the plate: Cut back on the very spicy, sour, salty, and fried foods eaten often, choose more cooling foods, and drink enough water. The texts tie palitya to heat, so a calmer plate is part of the care.
  • settle the heat of the mind: Because anger and constant worry are described as kindling inner heat, take a few quiet minutes of slow breathing or meditation each day. It supports the steady daily routine Ayurveda values.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Why hair greys early, the pitta-heat link Ayurveda describes, and the calm, cooling habits the old texts suggest, with honest words on what oiling and diet can and cannot do.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Ayurveda calls early greying palitya and links it mainly to excess pitta, the body's heat.
Anger, worry, very spicy or fried food, sun, and poor rest are the heat-raising habits the texts name.
Cooling habits and amla oiling may slow new greying, but no oil or diet turns silver hair black again.

glossary_terms.definition: The Ayurvedic name for greying or whitening of the hair.
The fiery principle of heat and transformation in the body, linked to early greying.
The practice of massaging the body and scalp with oil as part of daily care.
A rejuvenating substance or practice said to sustain vitality; amla is a classic one.
The recommended daily routine that keeps the doshas in balance.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, on palitya (greying) and its link to pitta and bodily heat
Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana, shiroroga section (palitya among pitta-related head and hair disorders)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Dinacharya, on abhyanga (daily oil massage)
Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu, Haritakyadi Varga, amalaki entry (amla as a cooling rasayana)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Palitya" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Palitya is defined as: The Ayurvedic name for greying or whitening of the hair.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to a cooling scalp oiling. Once or twice a week, warm a little coconut-based oil, ideally one infused with amla or bhringaraj, and massage it gently into the scalp. Leave it on for a while, then wash with lukewarm, not hot, water. This follows the old abhyanga custom of Ashtanga Hridaya.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to bring amla into your week. Eat fresh amla in season, or add it to your food in a simple home form. The Bhavaprakasha Nighantu prizes amla as a cooling rejuvenator that calms pitta, the dosha linked to early greying.
